title: do-while Loop
description: The loop that always runs its body at least once, checking the condition only after — and why that matters for menus and input validation.
objectives: Write a basic do-while loop with correct syntax
Understand the key difference from while: check-after vs check-before
Know a realistic use case where do-while is the natural fit
Avoid forgetting the semicolon after the while(condition)

sections.text: Notice the semicolon after while (condition) — it's required here, unlike the for and while loops we've already seen. Forgetting it is a common mistake.
Same output as while's version, because i <= 5 happened to be true from the start. The real difference only shows up when the condition is false from the beginning.
3. The Key Difference: Check-Before vs Check-After

rows: Checks the condition before running the body
Checks the condition after running the body
Body always runs at least once

sections.text: This is the entire point of do-while: even with a condition that's false from the very start, the body still executes exactly once before the check ever happens.

A menu needs to be shown at least once before there's anything for the user to respond to — there's no earlier point where checking the condition first would even make sense. This "do the thing, then check whether to repeat" shape is do-while's classic use case.

sections.title: Tasting a dish before deciding to add more salt
sections.text: You always taste the food first — that's not optional — and only after tasting do you decide: repeat (add more salt and taste again) or stop (it's good). while would be like deciding whether to taste before ever having tasted anything, which doesn't make sense. do-while guarantees the action happens first, then the condition decides whether it happens again.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the semicolon after while (condition); — required for do-while, unlike for and while.
Using do-while when zero executions should be possible — if the body should be skippable entirely, a plain while is the correct choice.
Assuming do-while and while always behave identically — they only differ when the condition is false on the very first check, but that difference matters.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the key difference between while and do-while?
questions.answer: while checks its condition before running the body, so the body may run zero times; do-while checks its condition after running the body, so the body always runs at least once.

questions.question: Give an example where do-while is a more natural fit than while.
questions.answer: Displaying a menu and asking the user to choose an option — the menu must be shown at least once before there's any input to check against a condition.

questions.question: What syntax detail is easy to forget in a do-while loop?
questions.answer: The semicolon after while (condition); at the end — it's required, unlike in for or while loops.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: do-while is like while, but it checks its condition after running the body instead of before, guaranteeing the body executes at least once — even if the condition turns out to be false on that very first check. This makes it a natural fit for situations like displaying a menu, where the action needs to happen before there's anything to evaluate. A do-while loop also requires a semicolon after its while (condition), which is easy to forget since neither for nor while need one.

sections.title: Trick to Remember do-while
sections.text: do-while = act first, ask later. while = ask first, maybe never act. Don't forget the semicolon after while(condition);.
